text.title: iac-patterns-cicd Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for iac-patterns-cicd. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: The CI pipeline MUST run: (1) fmt -check, (2) validate, (3) tflint, (4) security scan (checkov/tfsec), (5) plan on PR with comment output, (6) apply only on merge to main. Apply MUST require all previous stages to pass.
rule.rationale: Each stage catches a different failure class: fmt catches formatting drift, validate catches syntax errors, tflint catches provider-specific issues, security scan catches policy violations, and the plan review is the human gate before any cloud mutation.

rule.statement: Cloud authentication in CI MUST use OIDC (id-token: write permission + configure-aws-credentials with role-to-assume). NEVER store long-lived IAM access keys as CI secrets.
rule.rationale: OIDC tokens are short-lived (15 minutes) and scoped to the specific workflow run; a leaked token has minimal blast radius compared to a long-lived IAM key that persists until manually rotated.

rule.statement: Production apply jobs MUST use a GitHub Environments deployment gate requiring manual approval. Apply from main branch only; never allow direct apply from feature branches.
rule.rationale: An approval gate before production apply is the last human checkpoint; it prevents accidental production deploys from merged-but-unreviewd PRs.

rule.statement: Configure a scheduled drift detection job that runs terraform plan daily (or hourly for critical infrastructure) and alerts when plan output is non-empty. Do NOT auto-apply drift remediation — require human review of each drift incident.
rule.rationale: Auto-remediation of drift can destroy intentional manual changes made during an incident response. Alert and require human judgment for each drift detection event.
